title: Spatially resolved, highly multiplexed RNA profiling in single cells
authors: Kok Hao Chen, Alistair N. Boettiger, Jeffrey R. Moffitt, Siyuan Wang, Xiaowei Zhuang
sourceFile: /Users/augustsirius/Desktop/已清洗_260617/08b - MERFISH - spatially resolved highly multiplexed RNA profiling in single cells_cleaned.md
oneSentence: The authors developed MERFISH, a single-molecule imaging method that uses combinatorial labeling and sequential hybridization with error-robust encoding to simultaneously measure copy numbers and spatial localizations of 140 to 1001 RNA species in hundreds of individual human fibroblast cells.
orientation: An in situ, image-based spatial transcriptomics method paper. It most directly feeds Claim 2 (scRNA-seq loses spatial information -> spatial omics methods) and the spatial axis (Claim C), demonstrating that thousands of RNA species can be profiled while preserving subcellular and native spatial context. It also bears on the modality axis as a transcriptome-only (RNA) method, and can serve as a foil for the temporal axis (it is a single fixed snapshot of fixed cells, no time course). Its finding that RNA spatial distribution correlates with the function/localization of the encoded protein can be used both to support spatial information's value and to nuance modality complementarity.

excerpts.text: Knowledge of the expression profile and spatial landscape of the transcriptome in individual cells is essential for understanding the rich repertoire of cellular behaviors. Here, we report multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), a single-molecule imaging approach that allows the copy numbers and spatial localizations of thousands of RNA species to be determined in single cells.
excerpts.note: States MERFISH measures copy numbers AND spatial localizations of thousands of RNA species in single cells, framing spatial landscape as essential.

excerpts.text: System-wide analyses of the abundance and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells promise to transform our understanding in many areas of cell and developmental biology, such as the mechanisms of gene regulation, the heterogeneous behavior of cells, and the development and maintenance of cell fate (1).
excerpts.note: Asserts the importance of spatial organization of RNAs for understanding cell behavior and fate.

excerpts.text: Single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) has emerged as a powerful tool for studying the copy number and spatial organization of RNAs in single cells either in isolation or in their native tissue context (2, 3). Taking advantage of its ability to map the spatial distributions of specific RNAs with high resolution, smFISH has revealed the importance of subcellular RNA localization in diverse processes such as cell migration, development, and polarization (4–8).
excerpts.note: States smFISH preserves native tissue context and that subcellular RNA localization matters for migration, development, and polarization.

excerpts.text: However, application of the smFISH approach to many systemslevel questions remains limited by the number of RNA species that can be simultaneously measured in single cells. State-of-the-art efforts by using combinatorial labeling with either colorbased barcodes or sequential hybridization have enabled simultaneous measurements of 10 to 30 different RNA species in individual cells (16–19), yet many interesting biological questions would benefit from the measurement of hundreds to thousands of RNAs within a single cell.
excerpts.note: Concedes prior spatial/imaging methods were limited to 10-30 RNA species, motivating the throughput increase to hundreds-thousands.

excerpts.text: knowledge of the subcellular organizations of numerous RNAs and their correlations could help elucidate molecular mechanisms underlying the establishment and maintenance of many local cellular structures, and RNA profiling of individual cells in native tissues could allow in situ identification of cell type.
excerpts.note: Argues subcellular spatial organization of RNAs and in-situ native-tissue profiling enable cell-type identification and study of local cellular structures.

excerpts.text: We demonstrated this transcriptome imaging approach by simultaneously measuring 140 RNA species with an encoding scheme that can both detect and correct errors and 1001 RNA species with an encoding scheme that can detect but not correct errors.
excerpts.note: Reports demonstrated throughput of 140 genes (error-correcting) and 1001 genes (error-detecting) per single cell.

excerpts.text: We first imaged 140 RNA species in human fibroblast cells using MERFISH with 16 rounds of hybridization and a modified Hamming code capable of both error detection and correction. We obtained \~80% detection efficiency and observed excellent correlation of RNA copy numbers determined with MERFISH with both bulk RNA sequencing data and conventional smFISH measurements of individual genes.
excerpts.note: Reports ~80% detection efficiency for the 140-gene MHD4 measurement and good correlation with bulk RNA-seq and smFISH.

excerpts.text: In order to validate the copy numbers derived from our MERFISH experiments, we performed conventional smFISH measurements on 15 of the 130 genes, spanning the full measured abundance range of three orders of magnitude. For each of these genes, both the average copy number and the copy number distribution across many cells agreed quantitatively between our MERFISH and conventional smFISH measurements (fig. S8, A and B).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Cross-validates MERFISH copy numbers against an orthogonal gold-standard (smFISH) over three orders of magnitude of abundance.

excerpts.text: Given that the agreement between the MERFISH and conventional smFISH results extended to the genes at the lowest measured abundance (<1 copy per cell) (fig. S8B), we estimate that our measurement sensitivity was better than 1 copy per cell.
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Reports measurement sensitivity better than 1 copy per cell, i.e. without amplification bias.

excerpts.text: As a final validation, we compared the abundance of each RNA species averaged over hundreds of cells to those obtained from a bulk RNA sequencing measurement that we performed on the same cell line. Our imaging results correlated remarkably well with bulk sequencing results, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.89 (Fig. 2H).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 140 genes with MERFISH by use of a 16-bit MHD4 code
excerpts.note: Validates the imaging method against bulk RNA-seq (Pearson r = 0.89) on the same cell line.

excerpts.text: Next, we used an alternative MERFISH encoding scheme, which is capable of detecting but not correcting errors, to image 1001 RNA species in individual cells using only 14 rounds of hybridization. The observed RNA copy numbers again correlate well with bulk sequencing data. However, the detection efficiency is only one-third that of the error-correcting encoding scheme.
excerpts.note: Reports a throughput-vs-accuracy trade-off: scaling to 1001 genes via MHD2 drops detection efficiency to one-third of the error-correcting MHD4 scheme.

excerpts.text: As the degree of multiplexing is increased, it is important to consider the potential increase in the density of RNAs that need to be resolved in each round of imaging. On the basis of our imaging and sequencing results, we estimate that including the whole transcriptome of the IMR90 cells would lead to a total RNA density of \~200 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup>. Using our current imaging and analysis methods, we could resolve 2 to 3 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup> per hybridization round (38), which would reach a total RNA density of \~20 molecules/mm<sup>3</sup> after 32 rounds of hybridization. This density should allow all but the top 10% most expressed genes to be imaged simultaneously or a subset of genes with even higher expression levels to be included.
excerpts.note: Concedes a molecular-crowding/density limit: current methods cannot resolve the whole transcriptome simultaneously, only all but the top ~10% most expressed genes.

excerpts.text: Last, theoretical predictions (17) indicate that the use of superresolution imaging (41, 42) could increase the resolvable density to \~10<sup>5</sup> molecules/mm<sup>3</sup>, which should be ample to address the entire transcriptome, even in cell types with RNA densities substantially higher than that of IMR90. However, RNAs in densely packed structures, such as p-bodies and stress granules, may still elude measurement.
excerpts.note: Concedes that even with super-resolution, RNAs in densely packed structures such as P-bodies and stress granules may still escape measurement.

excerpts.text: As an imaging-based approach, MERFISH also allowed us to investigate the spatial distributions of many RNA species simultaneously. Several patterns emerged from the visual inspection of individual genes, with some RNA transcripts enriched in the perinuclear region, some enriched in the cell periphery, and some scattered throughout the cell (Fig. 4A).
excerpts.note: Demonstrates subcellular spatial distribution patterns (perinuclear, peripheral, scattered) are directly resolvable, which dissociation-based sequencing would erase.

excerpts.text: Group I contained genes encoding extracellular proteins such as FBN1, FBN2, and THSB1; secreted proteins such as PAPPA; and integral membrane proteins such as LRP1 and GPR107. These proteins have no obvious commonalities in function. Rather, a GO analysis showed significant enrichment for location terms, such as extracellular region, basement membrane, or perivitelline space (Fig. 4E). To reach these locations, proteins must pass through the secretion pathway, which often requires translation of mRNA at the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) (34, 35). Thus, we propose that the spatial pattern that we observed for these mRNAs reflects their cotranslational enrichment at the ER.
excerpts.note: Links mRNA subcellular spatial pattern to the localization/destination of the encoded protein (ER cotranslational enrichment), connecting RNA spatial position to protein-layer information.

excerpts.text: We further analyzed correlations in the spatial distributions of different RNA species and identified groups of RNAs with different distribution patterns in the cell.
excerpts.note: Reports that spatial distribution patterns of RNAs can be correlated and grouped across genes, a readout unavailable to dissociated sequencing.

excerpts.text: Such covariation analysis can constrain regulatory networks, suggest new regulatory pathways, and predict function for unannotated genes based on associations with covarying genes (11, 28).
excerpts.anchor: Analysis of expression covariation among different genes
excerpts.note: States that single-cell covariation analysis can constrain gene regulatory networks and predict function for unannotated genes.

excerpts.text: The groups identified here included 46 RNA species lacking any previous GO annotations, for which we can now hypothesize function on the basis of their group association (table S4).
excerpts.anchor: Measuring 1001 genes with MERFISH by use of a 14-bit MHD2 code
excerpts.note: Quantifies functional-hypothesis output: 46 unannotated RNA species assigned candidate functions via covariation grouping in the 1001-gene data.

excerpts.text: Given its ability to quantify RNAs across a wide range of abundances without amplification bias while preserving native context, we envision that MERFISH will enable many applications of in situ transcriptomic analyses of individual cells in culture or complex tissues.
excerpts.note: Frames MERFISH as in-situ transcriptomics that preserves native spatial context without amplification bias, in culture or complex tissues.

excerpts.text: This work focuses on mRNAs, which are enriched in the cytoplasm. To estimate the fraction of mRNAs missed by excluding the nucleus region, we used conventional smFISH to quantify the fraction of molecules found inside the nucleus for six different mRNAs species. We found that only 5 T 2% (mean T SEM across six RNA species) of these RNA molecules are found in the nucleus. Use of super-resolution imaging and/or optical sectioning could potentially allow individual molecules in these dense nucleus regions to be identified, which will be particularly useful for probing those noncoding RNAs that are enriched in the nucleus.
excerpts.note: Concedes a coverage limitation: dense nuclear regions had to be excluded, so nucleus-enriched and noncoding RNAs were largely not measured (modality/coverage caveat).

figuresNumbers.contains: 130 RNA species encoded + 10 misidentification controls; 16 rounds of hybridization; ~80% calling rate after error correction; ~192 encoding probes per RNA; ~400 cells across seven independent experiments.
985 words encode RNAs + 16 misidentification controls; 14 rounds of hybridization; ~94 encoding probes per RNA; ~200 cells in three experiments; calling rate ~1/3 (~30%) of the MHD4 code.
1->0 error rate 10% on average; 0->1 error rate 4% on average.

Two shuffled MHD4 codebooks Pearson r = 0.94 (P = 1e-53); MERFISH vs bulk RNA-seq (140-gene) r = 0.89 (P = 3e-39); 1001-gene high-confidence (73%) vs bulk r = 0.76 (P = 3e-133), remaining 27% r = 0.65 (P = 3e-33); 107 shared genes 1001-gene vs 140-gene r = 0.89 (P = 9e-30).
MERFISH/smFISH copy-number ratio 0.82 +/- 0.06 (mean +/- SEM across 15 genes); sensitivity better than 1 copy per cell.
MHD4: 95% of 130 words counted above control median, 91% above max control confidence ratio; MHD2: 77% above control median, 73% above max control confidence ratio.
140-gene: 7 correlated gene groups; 1001-gene: ~100 groups; 46 unannotated RNAs and 61 transcription factors/partially-annotated proteins given hypothesized functions.

Two spatial groups: group I perinuclear (secreted/membrane proteins, ER cotranslational), group II cell-periphery (actin-binding, cytoskeletal proteins); 15 +/- 1% of RNA molecules outside fixed focal plane; 5 +/- 2% of mRNA molecules inside nucleus.
IMR90 whole-transcriptome RNA density ~200 molecules/mm^3; resolvable 2-3 molecules/mm^3 per round, ~20 molecules/mm^3 after 32 rounds; super-resolution could reach ~10^5 molecules/mm^3.
32-bit MHD4 code with four or six 1 bits would address 1240 or 27,776 RNA species; with just 16 hybridizations a simple binary code addresses >64,000 species but at high misidentification.

flags: Metadata verified from text: DOI 10.1126/science.aaa6090, Science 348(6233):aaa6090, received 5 January 2015, accepted 19 March 2015, published online 9 April 2015 (lines 414, 420).

OCR quirks preserved verbatim in excerpts and figuresNumbers: '\~' for approximately, 'T' used where source means '+/-' (e.g. '0.82 T 0.06', '15 T 1%', '5 T 2%'), 'THSB1' likely THBS1, superscript tags like <sup>3</sup> in molecules/mm^3.

Scope note: This paper measures only the RNA/transcriptome modality (it is image-based smFISH); it carries no protein, PTM, metabolite, or interactome measurement. Useful as the spatial-transcriptomics exemplar (Claim 2 / axis C) and as a single-modality datapoint, not as multimodal evidence.

Temporal axis note: MERFISH here is performed on chemically fixed cells (4% paraformaldehyde) and is a destructive, single-snapshot measurement with no time course or lineage tracing; relevant as a foil for the temporal axis (Claim B / Claim 3) though the paper does not discuss time explicitly.

Read at source: ref #17 (Lubeck & Cai 2012, Nat. Methods 9:743-748) is the theoretical basis for the resolvable-density / super-resolution scaling claims and is worth first-hand citation if those numbers are used.
